reasoning.step: Define the object of interest: the anomalous (pairing) vertex function averaged over the Fermi surface, denoted $\Lambda_{\omega}$, which is the frequency- dependent linear response of the anomalous self- energy to an infinitesimal pair-creating source; the Matsubara frequency variable is $\omega$ (with $\omega = \pi T(2n+1)$ for integer $n$) and $T$ is the temperature.

Begin from the exact Bethe–Salpeter equation (BSE) for the anomalous vertex in the normal state: $\Lambda_{\mathbf{k}\omega} = \eta_{\mathbf{k}\omega} + \int_{\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\omega^{\prime}}\tilde{\Gamma}_{\mathbf{k}\omega;\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\omega^{\prime}}\,G_{\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\omega^{\prime}}\,G_{-\mathbf{k}^{\prime},-\omega^{\prime}}\,\Lambda_{\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\omega^{\prime}}$, where $\tilde{\Gamma}_{\mathbf{k}\omega;\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\omega^{\prime}}$ is the particle- particle irreducible four- point vertex (the BSE kernel), $G_{\mathbf{k}\omega}$ is the single- particle Green's function, and $\eta_{\mathbf{k}\omega}$ is the infinitesimal source profile; this BSE is the starting exact equation for locating pairing instabilities and is the formal basis for the downfolding procedure. Fig. 3

Partition the pair propagator $G_{\mathbf{k}\omega}G_{-\mathbf{k},-\omega}$ into a low- energy coherent (infrared, IR) piece and a high- energy incoherent (ultraviolet, UV) piece by introducing an intermediate cutoff energy $\omega_{c}$ with the hierarchy $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}\ll\omega_{c}\ll E_{\mathrm{F}}$ (where $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}$ is a typical phonon frequency and $E_{\mathrm{F}}$ the Fermi energy). Explicitly write $G_{\mathbf{k}\omega}G_{-\mathbf{k},-\omega} = \Pi_{BCS} + \phi_{\mathbf{k}\omega}$, where $\Pi_{BCS} = \dfrac{(z^{\mathrm{e}})^2}{\left(\frac{\omega}{z_{\mathrm{w}}^{\mathrm{ph}}}\right)^2 + \epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}^2}\Theta(\omega_{c} - |\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}|)$ is the coherent pair propagator expressed in terms of (i) $z^{\mathrm{e}}$, the quasiparticle residue from electron–electron interactions (frequency- and momentum- independent on the scales of interest), (ii) $z_{\mathrm{w}}^{\mathrm{ph}}$ the frequency- dependent quasiparticle weight due to electron–phonon coupling, and (iii) $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$ the linearized quasiparticle dispersion renormalized by electron interactions; $\phi_{\mathbf{k}\omega}$ is the regular, incoherent remainder. This separation is the central field- theoretic scale- separation assumption for downfolding.

State the two small- parameter approximations controlling the subsequent manipulations: (i) the adiabatic small parameter $\dfrac{\omega_{\mathrm{D}}}{E_{\mathrm{F}}}\ll 1$ (which renders Migdal- type vertex corrections from phonons small), and (ii) suppression of cross terms that mix UV incoherent propagators and long- wavelength dynamical Coulomb effects provided $\dfrac{\omega_{c}^2}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^2}\ll 1$, where $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$ is a characteristic plasmon frequency; these small ratios control the size of omitted terms and determine the domain of applicability of the downfolding.

Decompose the full BSE into low- and high- frequency sectors by defining $\Lambda_{L}$ for $|\omega|<\omega_{c}$ and $\Lambda_{H}$ for $|\omega|>\omega_{c}$, and write the coupled two- sector equations symbolically as: (i) $\Lambda_{L} = \eta_{\omega} + \tilde{\Gamma}_{LL}\Pi_{BCS}\Lambda_{L} + \tilde{\Gamma}_{LH}\phi\Lambda_{H} + O\!\left(\frac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}}\right)$ and (ii) $\Lambda_{H} = \tilde{\Gamma}_{HL}\Pi_{BCS}\Lambda_{L} + \tilde{\Gamma}_{HH}\phi\Lambda_{H} + O\!\left(\frac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}}\right)$, where subscripts indicate frequency sectors and the $O(\omega_{c}^{2}/\omega_{p}^{2})$ terms denote cross- sector contributions that are parametrically small when plasmons are gapped relative to $\omega_{c}$.

Justify neglecting the cross- sector terms: use the exact small- $q$, low- frequency asymptotic form of the dynamical Coulomb interaction in the screened (plasmon) channel, $W^{\mathrm{s}}_{q,\Omega} = \dfrac{4\pi e^{2}}{q^{2}}\dfrac{\Omega^{2}}{\Omega^{2} + \omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}}$, (where $q$ is momentum transfer and $\Omega$ is transfer frequency) to estimate the size of $\tilde{\Gamma}^{\mathrm{e}}\cdot\phi\cdot W^{\mathrm{ph}}$ cross terms; integrating this conservative plasmon- pole form over the UV incoherent phase- space leads to suppression by the factor $\dfrac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}}$, so that provided $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}\gg\omega_{c}$ these cross terms are negligible at the claimed order. [15]

Eliminate the high- energy sector $\Lambda_{H}$ perturbatively: since $\tilde{\Gamma}_{HH}\phi$ describes only non- singular UV processes and $\tilde{\Gamma}_{HL}\Pi_{BCS}$ is suppressed by the small cross- sector estimates, formally solve the second equation for $\Lambda_{H}$ in terms of $\Lambda_{L}$ and substitute back into the first, thereby integrating out high- frequency pair fluctuations to obtain an effective low- energy particle- particle- irreducible vertex $\tilde{\Gamma}^{\omega_{c}}$ acting only within $|\omega|<\omega_{c}$; the omitted terms are bounded by the small parameters $\dfrac{\omega_{\mathrm{D}}}{E_{\mathrm{F}}}$, $\dfrac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}}$, and $\dfrac{T}{\omega_{c}}$.

Project momenta onto the Fermi surface: argue that after high- energy elimination the effective vertex $\tilde{\Gamma}^{\omega_{c}}$ is regular on the scale of Fermi- surface variations and therefore may be averaged over Fermi- surface directions to produce frequency- only kernels; introduce the Fermi- surface average notation $\langle\cdots\rangle_{\mathrm{FS}}$ and define the Fermi- surface- averaged phonon- mediated interaction $\lambda_{\omega\omega'} \equiv -(z^{\mathrm{e}})^{2}N^{*}_{F}\langle W^{\mathrm{ph}}_{\mathbf{k}_{F}-\mathbf{k}'_{F},\omega-\omega'}\rangle_{\mathbf{k}_{F},\mathbf{k}'_{F}}$, where $N^{*}_{F}$ is the quasiparticle density of states and $z^{\mathrm{e}}$ the quasiparticle residue.

Perform the integration over momentum components perpendicular to the Fermi surface using the coherent pair propagator form and obtain the downfolded, frequency- only Bethe–Salpeter equation for the Fermi- surface- averaged anomalous vertex $\Lambda_{\omega}$: $$ \Lambda_{\omega} = \eta_{\omega} + \pi T\sum_{|\omega^{\prime}|<\omega_{c}}(\lambda_{\omega\omega^{\prime}} - \mu_{\omega_{c}})\frac{z_{\omega^{\prime}}^{\mathrm{ph}}}{|\omega^{\prime}|}\Lambda_{\omega^{\prime}} + O\!\left(\frac{\omega_{\mathrm{D}}}{E_{\mathrm{F}}},\frac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}},\frac{T}{\omega_{c}}\right), $$ where $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ is the coherent particle- particle- irreducible effective electron– electron interaction defined with respect to cutoff $\omega_{c}$, and $z_{\omega}^{\mathrm{ph}}$ is the phonon- induced quasiparticle weight factor; this is the explicit frequency- only BSE stated in the conclusion.

Linearize the downfolded BSE at the critical point to obtain the linearized gap (ME) equation for the gap function $\Delta_{\omega}$ at $T_{c}$ by noting that near the instability $\Lambda_{\omega}\propto\Delta_{\omega}/(T-T_{c})$ and the diverging prefactors cancel, resulting in: $$ \Delta_{\omega} = \pi T_{c}\sum_{|\omega^{\prime}|<\omega_{c}}(\lambda_{\omega\omega^{\prime}} - \mu^{*})\frac{z_{\omega^{\prime}}^{\mathrm{ph}}}{|\omega^{\prime}|}\Delta_{\omega^{\prime}} + O\!\left(\frac{\omega_{\mathrm{D}}}{E_{\mathrm{F}}},\frac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}}\right), $$ where $\mu^{*}\equiv \mu_{\omega_{c}}$; this reproduces the downfolded linearized ME equation with controlled error terms.

State the renormalization (scale- changing) identity for the pseudopotential: show that the physical, temperature- dependent two- quasiparticle scattering amplitude $\gamma_{T}$ at low $T$ and $T\ll\omega_{c}$ satisfies $\gamma_{T} = \dfrac{\mu_{\omega_{c}}}{1 + \mu_{\omega_{c}}\ln(\omega_{c}/T)}$, which implies the Bogoliubov–Tolmachev–Shirkov renormalization relation connecting $\mu$ at different cutoffs: $$ \mu_{\omega_{c}} = \frac{\mu_{\omega_{c}^{\prime}}}{1 + \mu_{\omega_{c}^{\prime}}\ln\frac{\omega_{c}^{\prime}}{\omega_{c}}}. $$ This relation follows from the requirement that the physical $\gamma_{T}$ be independent of the arbitrary separation $\omega_{c}$ and is the explicit connection used to move pseudopotentials between scales.

Summarize the domain of controlled validity explicitly: the downfolded, frequency- only BSE and the identification of $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ and $\lambda_{\omega\omega'}$ are controlled approximations provided (i) adiabaticity $\dfrac{\omega_{\mathrm{D}}}{E_{\mathrm{F}}}\ll 1$ holds so phonon-vertex corrections are $O(\omega_{\mathrm{D}}/E_{\mathrm{F}})$, (ii) plasmons are gapped so that $\dfrac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}}\ll 1$ and scale- separation $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}\ll\omega_{c}\ll\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$ can be chosen, and (iii) well- defined Fermi- surface quasiparticles (Landau Fermi liquid) exist; the neglected terms are explicitly the enumerated $O(\cdots)$ corrections in the displayed equations.

conclusion.title: Controlled low-energy downfolding and precise ME/BSE formulation

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: A controlled effective-field-theory downfolding of the particle-particle Bethe–Salpeter equation (BSE) to a frequency-only, Fermi-surface theory is established, yielding precise microscopic definitions of the Coulomb pseudopotential and the effective electron–phonon coupling and exact forms of the low-energy Cooper-channel equations up to specified small parameters. Concretely, the downfolded anomalous-vertex BSE for the Fermi-surface-averaged anomalous vertex $\Lambda_{\omega}$ is $$ \Lambda_{\omega} = \eta_{\omega} + \pi T \sum_{|\omega^{\prime}|<\omega_{c}} \big(\lambda_{\omega\omega^{\prime}} - \mu_{\omega_{c}}\big)\frac{z_{\omega^{\prime}}^{\mathrm{ph}}}{|\omega^{\prime}|}\,\Lambda_{\omega^{\prime}} + O\!\left(\frac{\omega_{\mathrm{D}}}{E_{\mathrm{F}}},\frac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}},\frac{T}{\omega_{c}}\right), $$ and the critical-gap (linearized ME) equation at $T_{c}$ is $$ \Delta_{\omega} = \pi T_{c}\sum_{|\omega^{\prime}|<\omega_{c}} \big(\lambda_{\omega\omega^{\prime}} - \mu^{*}\big)\frac{z_{\omega^{\prime}}^{\mathrm{ph}}}{|\omega^{\prime}|}\,\Delta_{\omega^{\prime}} + O\!\left(\frac{\omega_{\mathrm{D}}}{E_{\mathrm{F}}},\frac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}}\right), $$ where $\lambda_{\omega\omega'}$ is the Fermi-surface-averaged phonon-mediated interaction, $z_{\omega}^{\mathrm{ph}}$ is the frequency-dependent quasiparticle weight due to electron–phonon coupling, and $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ is the coherent, particle–particle-irreducible effective electron–electron interaction defined with respect to the low-energy cutoff $\omega_{c}$; the Bogoliubov–Tolmachev–Shirkov renormalization relation $$ \mu_{\omega_{c}}=\frac{\mu_{\omega_{c}'} }{1+\mu_{\omega_{c}'}\ln\!\frac{\omega_{c}'}{\omega_{c}}} $$ connects values at different cutoff scales. The derivation is controlled provided the small parameters $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}/E_{\mathrm{F}}$, $\omega_{c}^{2}/\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}$, and $T/\omega_{c}$ are small; in particular it requires a gapped plasmon ($\omega_{\mathrm{p}}\gg \omega_{c}$) and a separation $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}\ll\omega_{c}\ll\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$.

reasoning.step: Define the computational goal: compute the temperature- independent, low- energy pseudopotential $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ (and specifically its Fermi- energy scale value $\mu_{E_{F}}$) from high- order diagrammatic expansions of the two- quasiparticle scattering amplitude in the Cooper channel, where direct finite- order expansions at low temperature suffer from nested logarithmic growth $\propto [\ln(E_{F}/T)]^{N}$ at order $N$.

Describe the Variational Diagrammatic Monte Carlo (VDiagMC) starting point: set up a renormalized perturbative expansion for the uniform electron gas (UEG) by re- expressing the Coulomb interaction as a Yukawa interaction $v_{\mathrm{R}}(\mathbf{q}) = 4\pi e^{2}/(\mathbf{q}^{2} + \lambda_{\mathrm{R}})$ plus a series of counterterms; introduce an auxiliary expansion parameter $\xi$ (with physical results at $\xi=1$) so that observables are expanded as power series in $\xi$ with optimized choice of screening parameter $\lambda_{\mathrm{R}}$ to accelerate convergence.

Construct the power series for the physical, temperature- dependent dimensionless two- quasiparticle scattering amplitude $\gamma_{T}(\xi)$ at a chosen low temperature $T$ (and corresponding lowest Matsubara frequency $\omega_{0}=\pi T$) as $$ \gamma_{T}(\xi) \equiv [z^{\mathrm{e}}(\xi)]^{2}\frac{m_{e}^{*}(\xi)}{m}N_{F}\,\Gamma^{\mathrm{e}}(\xi) \equiv \gamma_{T}^{(0)} + \gamma_{T}^{(1)}\xi + \gamma_{T}^{(2)}\xi^{2} + \dots $$ where $z^{\mathrm{e}}(\xi)$ is the quasiparticle residue series, $m_{e}^{*}(\xi)$ the effective mass series, $N_{F}$ the appropriate density of states, and $\Gamma^{\mathrm{e}}(\xi)$ the four- point vertex series; compute these coefficients using high- order VDiagMC sampling of Feynman graphs with the optimized renormalized expansion.

Explain the convergence problem: at low $T$ the $N$-th order term in $\gamma_{T}(\xi)$ grows as $[\ln(E_{F}/T)]^{N}$ because of nested Cooper- ladder particle- particle bubbles, so direct summation at $\xi=1$ is numerically unstable and cannot yield the temperature- independent pseudopotential $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$.

Introduce the homotopic mapping between the temperature- dependent scattering amplitude $\gamma_{T}(\xi)$ and a temperature- independent pseudopotential series $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi)$ via the algebraic relation $$ \mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi) = \frac{\gamma_{T}(\xi)}{1 - \gamma_{T}(\xi)\ln(\omega_{c}/T)}. $$ This relation is chosen so that when expanded in powers of $\xi$ the logarithmic temperature- dependent counterterms present in $\gamma_{T}(\xi)$ are re- packaged into temperature- independent coefficients of $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi)$, thereby curing the low- $T$ divergence of partial sums.

Demonstrate the conversion step: show that expanding the right- hand side in powers of $\xi$ yields $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi) = \mu^{(0)}_{\omega_{c}} + \mu^{(1)}_{\omega_{c}}\xi +\mu^{(2)}_{\omega_{c}}\xi^{2} + \dots$, with explicit relations such as $\mu^{(0)}_{\omega_{c}}=\gamma^{(0)}_{T}$ and $\mu^{(1)}_{\omega_{c}}=\gamma^{(1)}_{T} + [\gamma^{(0)}_{T}]^{2}\ln(\omega_{c}/T)$, so that the $(\ln T)^N$ growth of $\gamma_T$ is canceled order- by- order in $\mu_{\omega_c}$; numerically, partial sums of the $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ series converge rapidly even as $T\to 0$ (illustrated by the partial- sum behavior vs $T$). Fig. 7

Apply conformal- map style resummation to the finite- order homotopic series $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi)$ to accelerate convergence and extend the domain of analytic continuation to $\xi=1$, using the assumption that $\gamma_{T}(\xi)$ (and hence $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi)$) is analytic in a domain containing $0$ and $1$; the practical protocol uses conformal mapping choices and standard series- acceleration steps (details and justification given in the Appendix) to obtain a converged estimate of $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi=1)$.

Rescale the converged $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ to the physically meaningful Fermi- energy scale using the Bogoliubov–Tolmachev–Shirkov renormalization relation $$ \mu_{E_{F}} = \frac{\mu_{\omega_{c}}}{1 - \mu_{\omega_{c}}\ln(\omega_{c}/E_{F})}, $$ thereby producing the final "bare" pseudopotential $\mu_{E_{F}}$ appropriate for use in the downfolded ME/BSE.

Record practical algorithmic details and numerical evidence: (i) construct high- order series expansions of $\Gamma^{\mathrm{e}}$, $z^{\mathrm{e}}$, and $m^{*}$ using renormalized Yukawa expansion in VDiagMC; (ii) form $\gamma_{T}(\xi)$ and map to $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi)$ via the algebraic relation; (iii) apply conformal- map resummation to $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi)$ and evaluate at $\xi=1$ to obtain $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$, then rescale to $\mu_{E_{F}}$; the resulting protocol cures the $(\ln T)^N$ growth of finite- order Cooper- ladder contributions and yields rapidly convergent estimates as illustrated in the paper. Fig. 14

conclusion.title: Variational DiagMC homotopic expansion and resummation protocol for Cooper-channel quantities

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: A practical computational protocol is developed and implemented that (i) constructs high-order series expansions of the quasiparticle scattering amplitude $\gamma_{T}(\xi)$ using an optimized renormalized Yukawa expansion within Variational Diagrammatic Monte Carlo (VDiagMC), (ii) forms a homotopic mapping to a temperature-independent pseudopotential series $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi)$ via the algebraic relation $$ \mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi)=\frac{\gamma_{T}(\xi)}{1-\gamma_{T}(\xi)\ln(\omega_{c}/T)}, $$ and (iii) applies conformal-map style resummation to obtain converged estimates of $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi=1)$ and then rescales to $\mu_{E_{F}}$ using the Bogoliubov–Tolmachev–Shirkov relation. This protocol cures the low-temperature $(\ln T)^N$ growth of finite-order Cooper-ladder contributions, produces rapidly convergent estimates of $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ (illustrated by partial-sum behavior versus $T$), and yields the numerical $\mu_{E_{F}}$ values reported.

reasoning.step: State the explicit small- parameter conditions underpinning controlled downfolding: (i) adiabaticity $\dfrac{\omega_{\mathrm{D}}}{E_{\mathrm{F}}}\ll 1$ (phonon energy small compared to electronic Fermi energy), (ii) scale separation $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}\ll\omega_{c}\ll\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$ (a high- energy cutoff $\omega_{c}$ that lies between phonon and plasmon scales), and (iii) existence of Landau Fermi- liquid quasiparticles (well- defined Fermi surface and finite quasiparticle residue $z^{\mathrm{e}}$).

Explain why each condition is required: (i) $\dfrac{\omega_{\mathrm{D}}}{E_{\mathrm{F}}}\ll 1$ ensures Migdal- theorem suppression of phonon-vertex corrections so $W^{\mathrm{ph}}$-based truncation is valid; (ii) $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}\ll\omega_{c}\ll\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$ allows integration out of high- energy incoherent pair propagators while ensuring dynamical plasmon effects do not feed back into the low- energy Cooper channel beyond controlled $O(\omega_{c}^{2}/\omega_{p}^{2})$ corrections; (iii) Landau quasiparticles guarantee that the coherent pair propagator $\Pi_{BCS}$ has the BCS- type logarithmic low- energy singularity exploited in the downfolding and in PCF scaling.

List and characterize regimes where the downfolding fails or requires the full-frequency-momentum BSE: (a) plasmon softening $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}\lesssim\omega_{c}$ (plasmon dynamics intrude on the Cooper window so cross terms are not suppressed), (b) strictly two- dimensional systems with gapless $\sqrt{q}$ plasmon dispersion (absence of plasmon gap invalidates the $ \omega_{c}^2/\omega_{p}^2$ suppression and spatial nonlocal screening is essential), (c) systems with emergent soft collective modes from strong correlations (soft bosonic modes at energies comparable to $\omega_{c}$ spoil scale separation), (d) very low electron density where $E_{F}\sim\omega_{D}$ (adiabatic parameter fails), and (e) materials in which Umklapp processes, multiband, or core- electron screening contributions are significant (e.g., many transition metals) making local Fermi- surface projection insufficient.

Quantify the nature of the breakdown: when any of the above conditions are violated, the $O\!\left(\frac{\omega_{\mathrm{D}}}{E_{\mathrm{F}}},\frac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}},\frac{T}{\omega_{c}}\right)$ error estimates no longer guarantee smallness, and dynamical, momentum- dependent screening or vertex corrections can qualitatively change the sign or magnitude of the low- energy Cooper- channel interaction; in such regimes the only controlled approach is to retain full momentum- frequency dependence in the BSE (or to develop an alternate nonperturbative low- energy EFT appropriate to the new emergent scales).

Provide practical guidance: for typical three- dimensional simple metals with $r_{s}\gtrsim 1$ the plasmon gap is large and the stated conditions are satisfied (so the downfolded EFT-ME is quantitatively accurate); for low- density, quasi- two- dimensional, flat- band, or strongly correlated systems caution is required and either full BSE calculations or specialized EFT extensions must be developed before applying the frequency- only downfolded equations.

conclusion.title: Identified validity limits and domain of applicability of the downfolded EFT-ME framework

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: The downfolded EFT-ME framework and the frequency-only BSE are valid and controlled under the following conditions and are quantitatively accurate when they hold: (i) adiabaticity $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}/E_{\mathrm{F}}\ll 1$ (small ionic-to-electron mass ratio), (ii) separation of cut-off scales $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}\ll\omega_{c}\ll\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$ (plasmon frequency $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$ large compared to the high-energy cutoff $\omega_{c}$), and (iii) well-defined Fermi-surface quasiparticles (Landau Fermi liquid). The framework fails or requires full BSE treatment when any of these conditions break down, notably when plasmons soften ($\omega_{\mathrm{p}}\lesssim\omega_{c}$), in two-dimensional systems with gapless plasmon modes, in systems with emergent soft collective modes from strong correlations, in materials with very low conduction-electron density ($E_{F}\sim\omega_{D}$), or when Umklapp and multiband/core-electron contributions to screening are significant (e.g., many transition metals).

reasoning.step: Start from the downfolded, frequency- only BSE established in conclusion 1 as the theoretical object to be validated: the frequency- only BSE approximates the full frequency- and momentum- dependent BSE by integrating out high- energy incoherent sectors under the assumptions $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}\ll\omega_{c}\ll\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$ and small $\omega_{D}/E_{F}$.

Specify the representative parameter values for the test: choose a conventional metallic density corresponding to $r_{s}=1.91916$ (a value representative of aluminum- like density) and an adiabatic ratio $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}/E_{\mathrm{F}} = 0.005$ (small), and set a cutoff $\omega_{c}$ satisfying $\omega_{D}\ll\omega_{c}\ll\omega_{p}$.

Solve numerically both the full, frequency- and momentum- dependent BSE (i.e., the Bethe–Salpeter equation with the above $\bar{\Gamma}^{\mathrm{e}}$ and $W^{\mathrm{ph}}$ kernels retained in their full dependence) and the downfolded, frequency- only BSE (the reduced equation with Fermi- surface projection and frequency cutoff) for the anomalous vertex and extract the superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ in both approaches (numerical solution method follows precursory Cooper flow or direct linearized gap solutions as described in the methods).

Compare the computed critical temperatures: report the numerical results from the model calculation that $T_{c}^{(\mathrm{full})}/T_{F} = 10^{-5.668}$ and $T_{c}^{(\mathrm{approx})}/T_{F} = 10^{-5.667}$, i.e., a relative difference of approximately $0.2\%$, demonstrating the quantitative accuracy of the downfolded frequency- only reduction for conventional- metal parameters under the stated scale- separation conditions. Fig. 5

Conclude validity domain for practical use: because the toy- model comparison exhibits sub- percent agreement for $T_{c}$ at representative metallic parameters ($\omega_{D}\ll\omega_{c}\ll\omega_{p}$ and moderate $r_{s}$), the frequency- only downfolding is quantitatively justified for conventional metals and can be used reliably in the ab initio workflow developed in the paper; note caveats that the approximation may fail when scale separation or plasmon gapping conditions are violated (see validity limits).

conclusion.title: Quantitative validation of the Fermi-surface downfolding

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: The downfolded, frequency-only BSE is quantitatively accurate for conventional metallic parameters: in a representative toy model (RPA-screened electron irreducible vertex plus a model phonon interaction at $r_{s}=1.91916$ and $\omega_{\mathrm{D}}/E_{\mathrm{F}}=0.005$) the superconducting critical temperature obtained from the full frequency- and momentum-dependent BSE, $T_{c}^{(\mathrm{full})}$, and from the downfolded frequency-only BSE, $T_{c}^{(\mathrm{approx})}$, differ by $\sim 0.2\%$ (numerical values reported in the comparison).

reasoning.step: Treat upstream results as given: (i) the controlled downfolded definitions of the pseudopotential $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$, its renormalization relation, and the frequency- only BSE are established (conclusion 1), and (ii) the VDiagMC homotopic expansion and resummation protocol for extracting $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ from high- order diagrammatics is available (conclusion 4); these results are starting points and are not rederived here.

Specify the quantity computed: the "bare" Coulomb pseudopotential at the Fermi- energy scale, denoted $\mu_{E_{F}}$, is defined as the pseudopotential $\mu_{\omega_{c}}$ renormalized (via the Bogoliubov–Tolmachev–Shirkov relation) to the scale $\omega_{c}=E_{F}$ (i.e., the value of the coherent particle- particle irreducible e– e interaction at the Fermi- energy scale); this is the microscopic Coulomb parameter entering the downfolded ME/BSE.

Explain computational procedure used to obtain $\mu_{E_{F}}(r_{s})$: (i) perform high- order VDiagMC calculations of self- energy, four- point vertex, quasiparticle residue $z^{\mathrm{e}}$, and effective mass $m_{e}^{*}$ in the UEG for each targeted Wigner–Seitz radius $r_{s}$; (ii) assemble the series for the two- quasiparticle scattering amplitude $\gamma_{T}(\xi)$ and map it via the homotopic relation $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi) = \dfrac{\gamma_{T}(\xi)}{1 - \gamma_{T}(\xi)\ln(\omega_{c}/T)}$ to obtain the temperature- independent series for $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi)$; (iii) apply conformal- map/resummation to the $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi)$ series to converge to $\mu_{\omega_{c}}(\xi=1)$ and then rescale to $\mu_{E_{F}}$ using the renormalization relation. Fig. 7

State the computed numerical results (with quoted systematic uncertainties) obtained by the above protocol for integer $r_{s}$ values $1\le r_{s}\le 6$ (these are direct outputs of the resummed VDiagMC calculations and the $\xi$-homotopy/resummation pipeline): - $r_{s}=1$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 0.28(1)$, - $r_{s}=2$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 0.53(2)$, - $r_{s}=3$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 0.77(5)$, - $r_{s}=4$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 1.0(2)$, - $r_{s}=5$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 1.3(2)$, - $r_{s}=6$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 1.8(8)$; these values appear in the paper's tabulation and figure and include systematic uncertainties arising from finite- order truncation and resummation choices. Fig. 4 Table I

Compare to prior approximations and highlight qualitative disagreements: note that these VDiagMC- based $\mu_{E_{F}}(r_{s})$ values are significantly larger than estimates from Morel–Anderson (MA) static Yukawa/Thomas–Fermi approximations and static- RPA in the moderate- density regime ($r_{s}\gtrsim 1$), and they contradict dynamic- RPA predictions which (in prior work) produced negative pseudopotentials at $r_{s}\gtrsim 2$; therefore the many- body, vertex- corrected VDiagMC results demonstrate the breakdown of MA and RPA approximations at moderate $r_{s}$ and provide a robust, density- dependent parameterization of the bare pseudopotential.

State practical usage: these $\mu_{E_{F}}(r_{s})$ values supply a concrete, first- principles input to the downfolded ME/BSE equations for simple metals after appropriate material- specific adjustments (e.g., accounting for band mass and conduction electron density), thereby replacing the ad hoc phenomenological choices of $\mu^{*}$ historically used in $T_{c}$ predictions.

conclusion.title: First-principles Coulomb pseudopotential µ* for the 3D uniform electron gas (numerical values)

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: High-order variational DiagMC calculations combined with a homotopic resummation yield the dimensionless "bare" Coulomb pseudopotential at the Fermi-energy scale, $\mu_{E_{F}}$, for the three-dimensional uniform electron gas (UEG) as a function of Wigner–Seitz radius $r_{s}$. For integer $r_{s}$ values $1\le r_{s}\le 6$ the extracted $\mu_{E_{F}}$ (with quoted systematic uncertainties from the VDiagMC and resummation procedure) are: - $r_{s}=1$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 0.28(1)$, - $r_{s}=2$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 0.53(2)$, - $r_{s}=3$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 0.77(5)$, - $r_{s}=4$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 1.0(2)$, - $r_{s}=5$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 1.3(2)$, - $r_{s}=6$: $\mu_{E_{F}} = 1.8(8)$. These values are significantly larger than estimates based on Morel–Anderson (static Yukawa/Thomas–Fermi) or static-RPA screening in the moderate-density regime ($r_{s}\gtrsim 1$) and disagree with dynamic-RPA predictions of negative pseudopotentials at $r_{s}\gtrsim 2$. The data provide a quantitative, density-dependent parameterization of $\mu_{E_{F}}$ usable in downfolded ME/BSE calculations for simple metals after adjusting for material band mass and electron density.

reasoning.step: Take as given the downfolded expression for the electron-phonon coupling entering the frequency- only ME/BSE: the effective quasiparticle electron–phonon matrix element is $g(\mathbf{k},\mathbf{q}) \equiv g^{(0)}_{\mathbf{q}}\dfrac{z^{e}}{\epsilon_{\mathbf{q}}}\Gamma_{3}^{e}(\mathbf{k};\mathbf{q})$, where $g^{(0)}_{\mathbf{q}}$ is the bare ion- induced coupling, $z^{e}$ the electronic quasiparticle residue, $\epsilon_{\mathbf{q}}$ the dielectric screening factor, and $\Gamma_{3}^{e}(\mathbf{k};\mathbf{q})$ the three- point electronic vertex correction; the effective dimensionless e– ph coupling $\lambda$ is obtained by a Fermi- surface average $\lambda = N_{F}\sum_{\kappa}\left\langle \dfrac{g_{\kappa}^{2}(\mathbf{k},\mathbf{q})}{\omega_{\kappa,\mathbf{q}}^{2}}\right\rangle_{\mathrm{FS}}$ with $N_{F}$ the quasiparticle density of states and $\omega_{\kappa,\mathbf{q}}$ phonon frequencies.

Introduce the DFPT (Kohn–Sham) ansatz for the screened ionic potential used in conventional ab initio calculations: $g^{\mathrm{KS}}(\mathbf{q}) \equiv \dfrac{g_{\mathbf{q}}^{(0)}}{1 - (v_{\mathbf{q}} + f_{\mathrm{xc}})\chi_{0}^{e}(\mathbf{q})}$, where $v_{\mathbf{q}}$ is the Coulomb potential, $f_{\mathrm{xc}}$ the (LDA) exchange–correlation kernel, and $\chi_{0}^{e}(\mathbf{q})$ the Kohn–Sham Lindhard response; $g^{\mathrm{KS}}(\mathbf{q})$ depends only on momentum transfer $\mathbf{q}$ (before projection onto Bloch orbitals). Fig. 8

Explain the numerical test performed: compute the many- body quantity $z^{e}\dfrac{v_{\mathbf{q}}}{\epsilon_{\mathbf{q}}}\Gamma_{3}^{e}(\mathbf{k};\mathbf{q})$ using VDiagMC for the UEG across densities $1\le r_{s}\le 5$ (evaluating $z^{e}$, dielectric screening $\epsilon_{\mathbf{q}}$, and the three- point vertex correction), and compare this many- body quasiparticle vertex product directly to the DFPT screened ionic potential $g^{\mathrm{KS}}(\mathbf{q})$ (no Bloch- state projection needed for UEG).

Report the numerical observation: despite sizeable separate interaction corrections to $z^{e}$, to the dielectric screening $\epsilon_{\mathbf{q}}$, and to the vertex $\Gamma_{3}^{e}$ individually, their product exhibits a near cancellation of interaction effects, yielding $$ z^{e}\frac{v_{\mathbf{q}}}{\epsilon_{\mathbf{q}}}\Gamma_{3}^{e}(\mathbf{k};\mathbf{q}) \approx \frac{v_{\mathbf{q}}}{1 - (v_{\mathbf{q}} + f_{\mathrm{xc}})\chi_{0}^{e}(\mathbf{q})} \equiv g^{\mathrm{KS}}(\mathbf{q}), $$ with only weak residual $\mathbf{k}$-dependence for momentum transfers relevant to Fermi- surface scattering $|\mathbf{q}|\le 2k_{F}$, and excellent quantitative agreement for $r_{s}\le 5$ (numerical comparison shown in the paper). Fig. 8

Conclude the practical implication: because the many- body quasiparticle e– ph vertex product is accurately reproduced by the DFPT/Kohn–Sham screened ionic potential in the UEG density range studied, one may use DFPT- computed matrix elements projected onto Bloch states as accurate proxies for the quasiparticle electron–phonon coupling entering $\lambda$ for simple metals (after accounting for the small difference between band and quasiparticle densities of states); hence DFPT is validated in this regime for computing the phonon- mediated kernel in the downfolded ME/BSE.

conclusion.title: DFPT accurately reproduces the quasiparticle electron–phonon vertex in UEG

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: For the uniform electron gas in the density range $1\le r_{s}\le 5$, the many-body quasiparticle electron–phonon vertex—expressed as the product of the quasiparticle residue, screened Coulomb interaction, and the three-point electronic vertex correction, $$ z^{e}\frac{v_{\mathbf{q}}}{\epsilon_{\mathbf{q}}}\Gamma_{3}^{e}(\mathbf{k};\mathbf{q}), $$ is numerically well approximated by the DFPT (Kohn–Sham) screened ionic potential $$ \frac{v_{\mathbf{q}}}{1-(v_{\mathbf{q}}+f_{\mathrm{xc}})\chi_{0}^{e}(\mathbf{q})}\equiv g^{\mathrm{KS}}(\mathbf{q}), $$ i.e., $g(\mathbf{k},\mathbf{q})\approx g^{\mathrm{KS}}(\mathbf{q})$ with only weak residual $\mathbf{k}$-dependence over the Fermi-surface-relevant momentum range $|\mathbf{q}|\le 2k_{F}$. This near cancellation of interaction corrections (quasiparticle weight vs vertex renormalization and dielectric screening) justifies the use of DFPT-derived matrix elements to compute the effective e–ph coupling $\lambda$ for simple metals.

reasoning.step: Treat upstream results as known: accept as given (i) the controlled downfolded frequency- only ME/BSE and microscopic definitions of $\mu^{*}$ and $\lambda$ (conclusion 1), (ii) numerical validation of the downfolding accuracy in conventional- metal parameters (conclusion 2), and (iii) the availability of first- principles many- body methods (VDiagMC) to compute $\mu_{E_{F}}$ and of DFPT to compute $g^{\mathrm{KS}}$ and hence $\lambda$ (conclusions 3 and 5); these upstream results are inputs to the workflow described here.

Outline the ab initio workflow sequence and required quantities: (i) compute many- electron correlation inputs using high- order diagrammatic many- body techniques (e.g., VDiagMC): the quasiparticle density of states $N_{F}^{*}$, quasiparticle residue $z^{e}$, the static Fermi- surface- averaged four- point vertex yielding $\mu_{E_{F}}$, and the three- point quasiparticle vertex $\Gamma_{3}^{e}$; (ii) compute phonon spectra and DFPT electron–phonon matrix elements $g^{\mathrm{KS}}(\mathbf{q})$ and derive $\lambda$ via Fermi- surface averaging of the projected matrix elements; (iii) construct the downfolded frequency- only BSE (EFT-ME) kernel using the microscopic $\mu^{*}$ and $\lambda$ definitions; (iv) determine $T_{c}$ and the gap function by computing the normal- state anomalous- vertex $\Lambda_{\omega}$ and applying the precursory Cooper flow (PCF) scaling $\Lambda_{0}(T) = [1 + g\ln(\omega_{\Lambda}/T)]^{-1}$ to infer $T_{c}$ from normal- state calculations rather than solving the ME equations directly at $T_c$. Fig. 9

Justify each workflow step with prior results and approximations: (i) VDiagMC with the homotopic/resummation protocol yields numerically controlled $\mu_{E_{F}}$ for UEG- like conduction electrons (conclusion 3); (ii) DFPT is validated as reproducing the quasiparticle- level electron–phonon vertex in the relevant density range (conclusion 5), justifying use of DFPT matrix elements for $\lambda$; (iii) the downfolded frequency- only BSE constructed from these microscopic inputs is accurate under the scale- separation conditions validated in conclusion 2; (iv) the PCF relation $\Lambda_{0}(T) = [1 + g\ln(\omega_{\Lambda}/T)]^{-1}$ allows extraction of $T_{c}$ from normal- state $\Lambda_{0}(T)$ computed at several temperatures above $T_{c}$, thereby enabling determination of ultra- low $T_{c}$ values inaccessible to direct solution at exponentially small temperatures. [25]

Explain the numerical procedure to extract $T_{c}$ in practice: (i) compute $\Lambda_{0}(T)$ (the low- frequency, Fermi- surface- averaged anomalous vertex) at several low temperatures $T\gg T_{c}$ but within the regime where PCF holds; (ii) fit $\Lambda_{0}(T)$ to the PCF functional form to extract the dimensionless coupling $g$ and the high- energy cutoff $\omega_{\Lambda}$; (iii) infer $T_{c} = \omega_{\Lambda}e^{1/g}$ (or equivalently the temperature at which denominator vanishes) and then solve the linearized ME gap equation if a frequency- dependent gap is required.

State the claimed capabilities of the workflow: it yields an end- to- end, parameter- free procedure to predict $T_{c}$ (including ultra- low $T_{c}$ and pressure- dependent suppression of superconductivity) by combining controlled many- body computation of $\mu^{*}$, validated DFPT computation of $\lambda$, and the PCF extrapolation from normal- state anomalous vertex calculations; this eliminates the historical need to choose $\mu^{*}$ phenomenologically.

conclusion.title: Ab initio workflow combining many-body µ* and DFPT λ with precursory Cooper flow

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: An end-to-end ab initio workflow is presented that (i) computes many-electron correlation inputs (quasiparticle density of states $N_{F}^{*}$, quasiparticle residue $z^{e}$, the static, Fermi-surface-averaged four-point vertex determining $\mu_{E_{F}}$, and the three-point quasiparticle vertex) using high-order diagrammatic many-body techniques (e.g., VDiagMC), (ii) computes phonon spectra and DFPT electron–phonon matrix elements $g^{\mathrm{KS}}(\mathbf{q})$ to obtain $\lambda$, (iii) constructs the downfolded frequency-only BSE (EFT-ME) kernel using the microscopic definitions of $\mu^{*}$ and $\lambda$, and (iv) determines $T_{c}$ and the gap function by computing the normal-state anomalous-vertex $\Lambda_{\omega}$ and applying the precursory Cooper flow (PCF) scaling $\Lambda_{0}(T) = [1+g\ln(\omega_{\Lambda}/T)]^{-1}$ to infer $T_{c}$ from normal-state calculations. This workflow enables prediction of ultra-low $T_{c}$ and quantum phase transition points without empirical tuning of $\mu^{*}$.

reasoning.step: Treat upstream inputs as established: use (i) the microscopic pseudopotential values $\mu_{E_{F}}(r_{s})$ computed by VDiagMC and resummation (conclusion 3), (ii) the validation that DFPT $g^{\mathrm{KS}}(\mathbf{q})$ accurately reproduces the quasiparticle electron–phonon vertex in the UEG regime (conclusion 5), and (iii) the ab initio workflow combining these inputs with the downfolded BSE and PCF extraction of $T_{c}$ (conclusion 6); these are the starting inputs for the material- specific calculations.

Describe mapping of real simple metals to UEG parameters: for each simple metal (Li, Na, K, Mg, Al, Zn) extract conduction- electron density and an effective band mass $m_{\mathrm{b}}$ from DFT band- structure calculations (explicit computational details: Quantum Espresso with ONCV pseudopotentials, PBE functional, Wannier interpolation etc.), compute the material Wigner–Seitz radius $r_{s}$ appropriate to conduction electrons and rescale $r_{s}\to (m_{b}/m)r_{s}$ when mapping to UEG pseudopotential table, and interpolate the VDiagMC $\mu_{E_{F}}(r_{s})$ results to obtain the material $\mu_{E_{F}}$; compute DFPT $g^{\mathrm{KS}}$ and obtain $\lambda$ via Fermi- surface averaging using EPW/Wannier interpolation. Fig. 9 Table II

Compute $T_{c}$ using the downfolded ME/BSE with microscopic inputs: substitute the computed $\lambda$ (from DFPT) and microscopic $\mu_{E_{F}}$ (from VDiagMC, rescaled for band mass) into the frequency- only BSE / linearized ME kernel, compute the normal- state anomalous vertex $\Lambda_{0}(T)$ at a series of low temperatures (above the expected $T_{c}$) and apply PCF scaling to extract $T_{c}$; when desired, solve the linearized gap equation at the obtained $T_{c}$ to obtain the gap function.

State the pressure- dependent prediction for Al and the quantum- critical observations: using pressure- dependent DFT lattice parameters and DFPT phonons combined with VDiagMC $\mu$ mapped to the pressure- adjusted conduction density, compute $T_{c}(P)$ and observe continuous suppression of $T_{c}$ with increasing pressure; predict that, assuming no structural transitions, s- wave superconductivity in Al is suppressed to zero at a critical pressure $\sim 60\,$GPa (with $T_{c}$ already below experimentally accessible values at tens of GPa). Note that Mg and Na are predicted to be proximate to a normal–superconducting critical point yielding log- divergent pair- field susceptibility $\chi\sim\ln T$ in a quantum- critical regime. Fig. 10 Fig. 11

Summarize the scientific consequence: these material- specific, fitting- free predictions—obtained by combining VDiagMC $\mu_{E_{F}}$, DFPT $\lambda$, and PCF extraction of $T_{c}$—resolve longstanding discrepancies for low- $T_{c}$ materials and provide experimentally testable predictions (e.g., Al pressure suppression, quantum- critical pair- susceptibility in Mg and Na), demonstrating the practical utility of the presented ab initio framework.

conclusion.title: Predictions and benchmarks for simple metals; pressure-driven suppression of superconductivity in Al

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: Applying the above workflow to a set of simple metals (Li, Na, K, Mg, Al, Zn) with conduction-electron parameters fit to UEG, computed DFPT $\lambda$, and VDiagMC $\mu_{E_{F}}$, yields fitting-free $T_{c}$ predictions that substantially reduce prior uncertainties for sub-Kelvin superconductors and reconcile theory with experiment in benchmark cases. Representative numerical predictions (from Table II and Figs. 10–11) include: (i) Al at ambient pressure: $T_{c}^{\mathrm{EFT}}\approx 0.96\,$K (compared to experimental $1.2\,$K); (ii) Zn: $T_{c}^{\mathrm{EFT}}\approx 0.874\,$K (experimental $0.875\,$K); (iii) Li (hcp): $T_{c}^{\mathrm{EFT}}\approx 0.03\,$K (experimental reported $4\times 10^{-4}\,$K, with structural uncertainties noted); (iv) Na and Mg are predicted to have extremely small $T_{c}$ (e.g., practically zero within current experimental reach); (v) under assumed structural stability, Al is predicted to undergo a pressure-induced suppression of s-wave superconductivity and to become non-superconducting above a critical pressure $\sim 60\,$GPa (the calculated $T_{c}$ decreases continuously with pressure and falls below experimentally accessible values already at tens of GPa). These results resolve significant discrepancies for low-$T_{c}$ materials and show that some metals are proximate to a normal–superconducting critical point where the pair-field susceptibility would display quantum-critical (logarithmic) behavior.
